perturbation.original_text: The Offer shall be open for acceptance from the time of commencement until a time that is not earlier than 3:30 p.m. (Tokyo time) on the 30t h Japan Business Day from and including the Launch Date (as adjusted pursuant to Section 2.02(f) below, the "Offer Period").
perturbation.changed_text: The Offer shall be open for acceptance from the time of commencement until a time that is not earlier than 3:30 p.m. (Tokyo time) on the 10t h Japan Business Day from and including the Launch Date (as adjusted pursuant to Section 2.02(f) below, the "Offer Period").
perturbation.explanation: Changing the offer period to 10 business days may be too short and could violate regulations concerning adequate time for shareholders to consider the offer. This timeline modification creates non-compliance because it reduces the time period shareholders have to consider the tender offer.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 14e-1(a) states that a tender offer shall remain open for at least twenty business days from the date such tender offer is first published or sent or given to security holders. Shortening the period to 10 business days may not provide sufficient time for shareholders to adequately evaluate the offer, potentially violating US securities laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.14e-1 - Unlawful tender offer practices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.14e-1 - Unlawful tender offer practices. CFR prev | next § 240.14e-1 Unlawful tender offer practices. As a means reasonably designed to prevent fraudulent, deceptive or manipulative acts or practices within the meaning of section 14(e) of the Act, no person who makes a tender offer shall: (a) Hold such tender offer open for less than twenty business days from the date such tender offer is first published or sent to security holders; provided, however , that if the tender offer involves a roll-up transaction as defined in Item 901(c) of Regulation S-K ( 17 CFR 229.901 (c)) and the securities being offered are registered (or authorized to be registered) on Form S-4 ( 17 CFR 229.25 ) or Form F-4 ( 17 CFR 229.34 ), the offer shall not be open for less than sixty calendar days from the date the tender offer is first published or sent to security holders; (b) Increase or decrease the percentage of the class of securities being sought or the consideration offered or the dealer's soliciting fee to be given in a tender offer unless such tender offer remains open for at least ten business days from the date that notice of such increase or decrease is first published or sent or given to security holders. Provided, however, That, for purposes of this paragraph, the acceptance for payment of an additional amount of securities not to exceed two percent of the class of securities that is the subject of the tender offer shall not be deemed to be an increase. For purposes of this paragraph, the percentage of a class of securities shall be calculated in accordance with section 14(d)(3) of the Act. (c) Fail to pay the consideration offered or return the securities deposited by or on behalf of security holders promptly after the termination or withdrawal of a tender offer. This para

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the offer period from 30 business days to 10 business days. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both state that this is a violation of SEC Rule 14e-1(a), which requires tender offers to remain open for at least 20 business days. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states: "(a) Hold such tender offer open for less than twenty business days from the date such tender offer is first published or sent to security holders." Therefore, the modified contract creates a direct contradiction of a clearly stated legal requirement.

perturbation.original_text: For […***…] ([…***…]) years from and after the date of this Agreement, the Company will hold and treat in confidence, and will not use, and will cause their Affiliates to hold and treat in confidence, all non-public documents and information (including any information with regard to terms and conditions of this Agreement) concerning Acquiror and each of its respective Affiliates, except to the extent that such documents and information (1) are required or requested (with prompt notice of such request to be made to Acquiror) to be disclosed by applicable Law or any Governmental Authority, (2) generally become available to the public through no fault of the Company, (3) become available to the Company on a non-confidential basis, or (4) are independently developed by the Company or its Affiliates without reference to the furnished information.
perturbation.changed_text: For one (1) year from and after the date of this Agreement, the Company will hold and treat in confidence, and will not use, and will cause their Affiliates to hold and treat in confidence, all non-public documents and information (including any information with regard to terms and conditions of this Agreement) concerning Acquiror and each of its respective Affiliates, except to the extent that such documents and information (1) are required or requested (with prompt notice of such request to be made to Acquiror) to be disclosed by applicable Law or any Governmental Authority, (2) generally become available to the public through no fault of the Company, (3) become available to the Company on a non-confidential basis, or (4) are independently developed by the Company or its Affiliates without reference to the furnished information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may not be sufficient to protect trade secrets under applicable laws, particularly if the trade secrets have a longer useful life or the applicable statute of limitations for misappropriation is longer. Some jurisdictions offer trade secret protection beyond one year.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act provides for the protection of trade secrets. Trade secrets can retain their protection indefinitely, as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. A one-year confidentiality clause may be insufficient to protect trade secrets that have a longer lifespan, effectively undermining the protection afforded by the DTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The original text specified a confidentiality period of "[…***…] ([…***…]) years," while the changed text reduces this to one year. The Law Explanation argues that this one-year period may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), because trade secrets can retain their protection indefinitely as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. Thus, reducing the confidentiality period introduces an inconsistency regarding the time-sensitive obligations related to trade secret protection, and weakens the protection offered to the acquirer. This falls under the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Settlement Date" shall mean the 5t h Japan Business Day following the last day of the Offer Period, except as such date may be adjusted pursuant to Section 2.02(f).
perturbation.changed_text: "Settlement Date" shall mean the 10t h Japan Business Day following the last day of the Offer Period, except as such date may be adjusted pursuant to Section 2.02(f).
perturbation.explanation: Changing the settlement date to the 10th business day increases the time between the offer period ending and the settlement. Delaying payment beyond a reasonable period could violate tender offer regulations, especially if it is not properly disclosed or justified.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 14e-1(c) requires a person who makes a tender offer to pay the consideration offered or return the securities deposited by or on behalf of security holders promptly after the termination or withdrawal of a tender offer. Delaying settlement to the 10th business day, without adequate justification or disclosure, may violate the requirement for prompt payment or return of securities.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the settlement date to the 10th business day, which contradicts Rule 14e-1(c) requiring prompt payment or return of securities after a tender offer's termination. The law snippet 1 states, "Fail to pay the consideration offered or return the securities deposited by or on behalf of security holders promptly after the termination or withdrawal of a tender offer." Increasing the settlement date from the 5th to the 10th business day introduces a delay that could violate the 'promptly' requirement, making the modified contract non-compliant.
